content.problemStatement:
This is the easy version of the problem. The only difference between this version and the hard version is that here, vertices can be visited by the walk at most two times.

You're given a tree (a simple connected acyclic graph) with NNN vertices.

A walk in the tree is a sequence of vertices (w1,w2,‚Ä¶,wk)(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_k)(w1‚Äč,w2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,wk‚Äč) such that there exists an edge in the tree between wiw_iwi‚Äč and wi+1w_{i+1}wi+1‚Äč for each 1‚Č§i<k1 \le i \lt k1‚Č§i<k.

Note that a walk is allowed to have repeated vertices.

The walk (w1,w2,‚Ä¶,wk)(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_k)(w1‚Äč,w2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,wk‚Äč) is said to be valid if it satisfies the following properties:


w1=1w_1 = 1w1‚Äč=1 and wk=Nw_k = Nwk‚Äč=N, i.e. the walk starts at 111 and ends at NNN.
wi‚Č†Nw_i \ne Nwi‚ÄčÓÄ†=N for each 1‚Č§i<k1 \le i \lt k1‚Č§i<k, i.e. the walk never visits NNN except at the last step.
Each vertex appears at most twice\red{\text{twice}}twice in the walk.

Formally, for each vertex vvv (1‚Č§v‚Č§N1 \le v \le N1‚Č§v‚Č§N), there are at most two\red{\text{two}}two indices iii such that wi=vw_i = vwi‚Äč=v.
Count the number of valid walks in the given tree.

The answer can be large, so report it modulo 998244353998244353998244353.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN ‚ÄĒ the number of vertices in the tree.
The next N‚ąí1N-1N‚ąí1 lines describe the edges. The iii-th of these N‚ąí1N-1N‚ąí1 lines contains two space-separated integers uiu_iui‚Äč and viv_ivi‚Äč, denoting an edge between uiu_iui‚Äč and viv_ivi‚Äč.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: the number of good walks, modulo 998244353998244353998244353.
constraints: The input edges describe a tree on {1,2,‚Ä¶,N}\{1, 2, \ldots, N\}{1,2,‚Ä¶,N}.
The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2‚čÖ1052\cdot 10^52‚čÖ105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The only valid walk is (1,2)(1, 2)(1,2).
Test case 222: There are two valid walks, namely:

(1,2,4)(1, 2, 4)(1,2,4)
(1,2,1,2,4)(1, 2, 1, 2, 4)(1,2,1,2,4)
